interest: This reflects the interest of low-SES pupils and their advocates (equity civil groups, reform researchers) in ensuring the reform is not oversold, since a bounded gain reframed as a system-wide fix would legitimise a still-stratifying system while their constituents' core disadvantage — never applying at all — persists.
value: Equity (honest, scope-accurate) versus political convenience/excellence-framing that would let a marginal fix substitute for structural change.
fear: The real fear is that S1 gets sold as 'the equity reform', closing the political window on deeper structural change while the children who never apply — typically rural Roma and the lowest-SES families — remain invisible and unhelped.
affected: low-SES pupils who never apply to selective schools
assumption: The claim rests on the assumption that a meaningful share of equity loss occurs at the pre-application stage (self-selection out) and is therefore untouched by any mechanism operating only at the point of entry.
empirical_uncertainty: The directional fact — that S1 operates only on those who already apply and cannot reach non-applicants — is essentially settled by the mechanism's own design; the contested part is the magnitude of the prior-stage self-selection effect, which is not quantified in the inputs.
